data.title: WGMI: When Is It Better Than Bitcoin? Maybe Not Today
data.description:
CoinShares Bitcoin Mining ETF (NASDAQ:WGMI) offers an intriguing direct exposure to the shares of mining companies. I underline intriguing, because the management does not simply allocate resources with a market-weighted exposure in public companies in North America, but actively selects the most representative ones.

The problem is the cyclicality, in my view extreme, of the sector it operates in, which makes WGMI a speculative and/or tactical instrument for certain market phases;

So the question is: are we in “those” market phases? Yes, the cycle is positive, but the irrationality of the market lies in pricing WGMI’s holdings as growth, when they have a very divergent business model. Let me explain better.

What does WGMI do

With its $225M of AUM, it invests at least 80% in companies that derive at least 50% of their revenues or profits from Bitcoin mining activity, excluding direct investment in Bitcoin BUT (attention) leaving up to 20% the possibility of investing in companies that hold BTC on their balance sheet (and up to 5% exposure in bonds of these companies). It is therefore a pure play mining ETF that offers this service at a TER of 0.75%.

Its composition

WGMI has less than 30 holdings, where the top 3 positions cover 43% of the portfolio, which gives it a unique verticality towards the Bitcoin mining sector. Here is its top 10 holdings:

But let’s remember that the fund has a portfolio turnover of over 45%, as it is actively managed, and therefore rotates its holding distribution based on opportunistic logics.

A recognition to WGMI

If there is one thing to recognize about CoinShares, it is that the strategy, although not complicated from the prospect, captures opportunities in the sector well when they arise, and although less distributed in number of holdings, I consider it a step above competitors (FDIG), but also compared to stock picking.

For example, it well intercepted the expansion of IREN: a company that went from a loss in 2024 to an accounting net income of $86.9M, with F25 revenue growing 168% YoY thanks to the development of its contracted renewable capacity in the USA and Canada, and indeed the AI cloud expansion.

And note well, it has not always been among the top picks of WGMI. And in this sector, I think we all agree that it is not enough to simply take the top picks of the last halving cycle and put them in a portfolio.

A dynamic approach to WGMI

The monitoring drivers are always the same: energy-saving capacity – mining capacity – and % of product holding.

These are 3 components that give extreme cyclicality to mining companies; simply because they are based on a continuous demand for CAPEX but with scalability limited by the halving reward cut and therefore by the increase in hash rate.

Then, having BTC in holding, (in theory) companies expand the % of net income (accounting) in bull markets, and expand the guidance, and then shrink these variables in bear markets.

For example, RIOT, one of the main holdings of WGMI, is an easy example to bring up: it held in total HODL for 13 months (Feb 2024–Mar 2025), moving to positive accounting net income. But if you exclude BTC revaluation, RIOT remained operationally in loss (even without derivatives and settlement cost, -$50M). Then, it so happened that in Q2 2025 the company sold most of the production (Apr–Jun).

But this move theoretically exposed the company to strong cyclicality due to BTC fluctuations. Just like a precious metals mining company with gold. In practical terms, during bull markets it outperforms, because the market adjusts expectations (which were extremely low), and in bear markets instead it does the exact opposite.

Bitcoin

Following the same logic used so far, WGMI’s holdings will certainly be closely connected with the Bitcoin cycle. And it is logical to think that in order to predict WGMI’s performance, one must be able to interpret the phase in which BTC is positioned.

And as you will have understood from my research on Bitcoin, I maintain an optimistic view, especially looking at the long term. Simply, the institutionalization process, at the base of which there is the GENIUS Act. But looking more in the short term, simply also the Fed’s move. Monetary easing with inflation still above target lowers the real yield of US Treasuries, makes the dollar weaker, and strengthens the role of scarce assets such as BTC and gold.

Risk

Miners should outperform Bitcoin in bull markets like gold miners, the problem is the cyclicality of the sector, which shows up in bear markets, and let me say … in bull markets people don’t talk much about bear markets.

Beyond the fact that they are a highly energy-intensive sector, the problem is technological obsolescence and the halving, which respectively increase CAPEX and impact miners’ profitability. If you assume a growing hash rate, higher competition, margins remain compressed even while the price of BTC increases, but if the price goes up, the P/E goes up too, which typically in cyclical companies tends to remain more contained.

Well, today it is above 39x, but this is unusual for a cyclical sector, it is as if the market uses multiples (relative valuations) typical of a growth stock, when the accounting features would not place it in this category. Which is why, in my opinion, exposing yourself to WGMI today does not ensure you a wide margin of safety, and exposes you to a more aggressive rotation risk, which makes it less attractive than its underlying commodity, Bitcoin.

Conclusion

I think the business model of miners makes WGMI an extraordinarily high-beta tactical allocation instrument for the portfolio, but not a strategic fund in a portfolio perspective, as in my opinion the underlying, Bitcoin, could be instead. Today there is no concrete reason to assume the start of a negative cycle, but the lack of a “margin of safety” in WGMI makes it less attractive. Which is why I think the right rating is HOLD, or rather HODL.

data.title: The 3 Dividend Stocks That Account For 44% Of My Income
data.description:
Introduction

Are any of my readers fans of the show "The Office?"

The Office is a mockumentary sitcom that aired from March 2005 to May 2013. I'm pretty sure most of you have heard of it, as it was named one of the 100 greatest television shows of all time.

Now, the only reason I'm telling you this is that it includes the funny quote below:

"Whenever I'm about to do something, I think, "Would an idiot do that?" And if they would, I do not do that thing.”

- Dwight Schrute

As ridiculous as that statement above may be (after all, it was meant for a comedy show, not a financial article), it's something I often think about, whether you can believe it or not. Admittedly, I am someone who is quick to judge, which is a character flaw I have to work on. However, I am also my own biggest critic, especially when it comes to my job and portfolio.

See, among many other reasons, it's because I have invested every penny of my liquid net worth in my portfolio and because, as I have written in prior articles, I will almost be entirely depending on generating my own income once I decide to retire in the future (whenever that may be).

There is simply no room for error.

With that in mind, most of my readers also know I am not a very conventional investor, as my portfolio is far from "average." Although I have made it very clear in the past that I will improve my diversification at some point, I have always said that I will build my wealth around core positions instead of buying a few shares of every company I like.

Personally, I dislike small positions. For example, I would rather invest $2,000 (or whatever) in an existing position instead of opening a new investment. After all, what's the point of making $2,000 if I'm right and the stock doubles?

The downside is that a few companies dominate my portfolio. This is a serious risk to keep in mind, as a 16-stock portfolio where the biggest three holdings account for 40%, tends to lose correlation with the market sometimes.

Moreover, while I am not yet focused on income, selling some of my smaller holdings to fund my real estate investment in Saranda, Albania, has created some heavyweights that truly dominate my total portfolio income.

To give you an idea of what we're dealing with here, my portfolio has a 1.5% dividend yield if we exclude special dividends from companies like CME Group (CME) and Texas Pacific Land Corp. (TPL). This is roughly 20 basis points below the yield of the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), as my biggest four holdings, accounting for nearly 50% of my portfolio, all yield less than 1.0%. So, yeah, that tends to leave a mark.

Now, after working my way through the numbers in Excel, I found that just three companies account for 44% of my entire dividend income, which is a mindblowing number that will require a lot of new capital and patience (dividend growth from others) to straighten.

However, that's OK, as I fully trust these three companies.

It's also why I am writing this article. I will walk you through these three investments, highlight their strengths, weaknesses, and use current events and developments to incorporate the Big Picture we discuss daily.

So, as we have a lot to discuss today, let's get to it!

Antero Midstream (AM)- A High-Quality 5.0% Yield With Massive Free Cash Flow Potential

Antero Midstream is a C-Corp midstream company that does not issue a K-1 form. That's why I was able to buy it, as I'm a non-American investor unable to buy Master Limited Partnerships. However, even if I could buy MLPs, I would still have bought Antero Midstream, as the company is one of my favorite high-yield stocks on the entire stock market. Period.

Generally speaking, I am very bullish on midstream companies. In fact, I bring these stocks up multiple times per week, as they perfectly fit my thesis:

  • They provide high-quality income with limited exposure to commodity prices due to mostly fixed-fee contracts.
  • They benefit from long-term growth in oil and gas demand (and production).
  • Places like the Permian Basin offer tremendous growth opportunities due to a lack of natural gas and natural gas liquids pipelines.
  • After years of aggressive CapEx, most companies are now focused on shareholder distributions, debt reductions, and smaller growth projects.

Having said all of this, Antero Midstream isn't like its major peers, as it mainly exists to manage the midstream assets of Antero Resources (AR). Antero Resources is one of America's largest natural gas producers with very deep reserves and extremely low breakeven prices in the Appalachian Basin.

As a result, Antero Midstream isn't the kind of company that will announce a major takeover or a pipeline project in the Permian. Its operations will stay close to the needs of Antero Resources.

But that's OK, as this relationship provides massive opportunities, including high returns on invested capital, as the company has more planning stability. Also, because of the absence of a major project, it has one of the best balance sheets, the lowest reinvestment rates, and the highest cash availability among all of its peers, as we can see below.

In recent years, it has pushed its return on invested capital to nearly 20%, one of the highest numbers in the entire industry. This has helped the company boost adjusted EBITDA by 17% per year over the past 11 years.

Moreover, despite super-low natural gas prices, it boosted its adjusted EBITDA by 11% in 2Q25, supported by record gathering and processing volumes.

The best thing, however, is that the company has a lot of excess cash.

As we can see below, the company aims to spend just $180 million on CapEx this year and less than $200 million on interest. This leaves $300 million in post-dividend free cash flow used to buy back shares.

Going forward, analysts expect $840 million in free cash flow by 2027. This translates to a free cash flow yield of roughly 10%. As AM yields 4.9%, we're dealing with a future cash payout ratio of 50%, which leaves tremendous room for buybacks and dividend growth down the road.

After cutting the dividend in 2021 to fund operations (it won't have to cut again), it is focused on buybacks instead of dividend growth. Given that buybacks support future dividend growth, I think that's an excellent strategy.

The other day, a reader said something along the lines of "The moment AM management stops buying back stock, I will stop buying as well."

I fully agree with that and will keep gradually expanding my position as long as the company yields close to 5.0%.

The same goes for the next company.

Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ)- Hands Down One Of My Favorite Oil Income Plays

Just like Antero Midstream, Canadian Natural is also an energy stock. However, unlike its midstream "peer," it is directly dependent on commodity prices, as it's Canada's largest oil producer and second-largest natural gas producer.

It's one of the companies I have brought up quite a lot in recent months, including in articles like the one titled "I'm Convinced Energy Is The Most Misunderstood Investment Opportunity Of The Decade."

While I cannot tell you how much energy stocks annoy me right now due to subdued oil prices, daily price-moving headlines, and economic uncertainty (all under a thick layer of volatility), I love the risk/reward, as I see improving oil and gas demand, pressure on supply growth, valuation benefits, the fact that everyone seems to be bearish, and secular growth from data centers.

One of my favorite investments here is Canadian Natural. While it may be volatile and dependent on fossil fuel prices, it has qualities that others cannot match. This includes unmatched reserves in Canada's oil sands. Although these operations come with high upfront costs, they have no decline risks, no reservoir risks, and reserve lives of more than 40 years.

See, while I like quite a few American onshore drillers, think of Diamondback Energy (FANG), for example, these companies need to maintain high CapEx to keep production volumes flat, while struggling with falling Tier 1 reserves.

Canada, in general, is now in a great spot, as its biggest competitor is running out of its best reserves, OPEC is exhausting excess supply, and pricing could be a huge benefit going forward. That's why I like Canadian energy so much, despite a clear lack of support from the government in various areas.

With that said, Canadian Natural is breakeven in the low-$40 WTI range, which allows it to remain profitable even when others are forced to cut output. This makes its financials very resilient, even if its stock price takes the occasional hit. It also supports its dividend. Currently yielding 5.3%, the dividend has been raised for 25 consecutive years with a 21% CAGR since inception.

Its five-year dividend CAGR is even higher at 23.1%.

Furthermore, as I wrote in a recent article, I expect the company's payout ratio to rise to 100% in 2027 (or earlier), as it has currently reduced the payout ratio to lower post-acquisition debt after it has bought a number of oil sand assets to improve its production profile, which is a move I really like.

To give you an idea of how much payout potential it has (using the overview below), at $85 WTI, a number I expect to see next year, the company has the ability to generate close to C$7 in free cash flow per share, roughly 16% of its current share price.

Based on that number alone, I believe CNQ has what it takes to double in the years ahead and maintain strong dividend growth, making it one of my favorite income stocks, even though it may be the wrong choice for investors who hate being dependent on the price of oil (which I would understand).

Even at current oil prices, CNQ trades at just 6.4x operating cash flow, a mile below its longer-term average of 8.2x, indicating even a small upswing in sentiment could produce a total return of nearly 50%.

The next pick is a bit more controversial among readers.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) - Here, Opportunities Make Up For The Risks

I like "mission-critical" real estate, as it gives me a hard asset that others depend on. In the case of Rexford, we're dealing with a company that owns 421 industrial properties in Southern California's infill markets.

Although many readers (often rightfully) bring up the political issues with this market, this market has benefits:

  • It would be the 12th-largest economy if it were a country.
  • With a population of 24 million people, a $2.0 trillion gross domestic product, and the largest industrial base in the country, it requires a lot of real estate for consumer and industrial purposes.
  • The Port of Long Beach/LA accounts for more than a third of all U.S. port volumes. Roughly half of these volumes remain in the SoCal area.
  • Because the market is wedged between the ocean and mountains, with a series of strict zoning rules that favor conversion from industrial to residential assets, it has highly favorable supply risks. I believe SoCal has the best supply profile of all markets in the U.S. on a long-term basis.

Another reason why I like this company is that it has a market share of less than 3.0% in a market that mainly consists of buildings constructed in the first three decades after the Second World War. Most of these are owned by Mom and Pop owners. Through off-market deals, the company seeks to find value, often followed by renovation works to increase the return on its investment.

Even better, after tough post-pandemic years of exploding new supply (nationwide) and higher rates, momentum is in the company's favor, as it noted higher-than-usual leasing momentum in July and August during this month's Bank of America Global Real Estate Conference.

Moreover, the company, which has a BBB+ credit rating, one step below the A range, sees close to $200 million in embedded net operating income growth, which translates to a growth rate of 28%. Most of this is expected to come from in-place portfolio rent escalators of 3.7% and repositioning deals. Roughly $20 million comes from portfolio mark-to-market rent adjustments, a number I expect to grow substantially in the years ahead, as market rents bottom.

On top of that, last month, it was reported that Elliott Management had taken a stake in Rexford, likely to solve its recent underperformance issue by focusing more on shareholder returns. I expect more details on this story in the weeks ahead.

For now, I happily collect its dividend. Currently, REXR yields 4.0% with a five-year CAGR of 16.2%. Because of the 85% payout ratio and challenging market environment, dividend growth has come down to single digits. The same goes for its much larger peer, Prologis (PLD).

The good news is that improving market fundamentals, lower short-term rates, and the company's favorable valuation have supported its share price.

Currently, shares are up more than 40% from their 52-week lows, with a lot more room to run, as REXR trades at just 21.2x AFFO (adjusted funds from operations), below its peer average of 25.0x. Conservatively speaking, I see a path to $55-$60 per share, at least 30% above the current price.

If I'm right about rebounding inflation and cyclical growth, I believe we will see a return to what REXR has done before 2021, which is consistent outperformance and double-digit dividend growth.

Takeaway

Getting more than 40% of one's dividend income from three stocks isn't something I would advise anyone, especially not people who are dependent on income during retirement.

In my case, it happened because I shifted my focus to dividend growth in high-growth areas like landowners, AI infrastructure, and aerospace. When adding that I sold some income positions to finance real estate, we get a portfolio that is heavily imbalanced when it comes to total exposure and income generation.

The good news is that I rely on some of the highest-quality stocks on the market, which lets me sleep just fine. Today, we discussed three of my favorite income picks, which are all companies I expect to keep performing well and even beat the market.

While they are essential to more than 40% of my dividend income, they have the fundamentals to back it up, which is why I will keep adding to them, as I think of them as "never sell" investments, especially if I decide to retire at some point in the future.

Risks To Keep In Mind

Canadian Natural Resources is highly dependent on oil and gas prices. Although low breakeven prices and a healthy balance sheet protect its dividend even against severe mayhem, it could still pressure its stock price.

For Antero Midstream, the biggest risk is an environment where natural gas prices are so low that Antero Resources cuts its output. However, as Antero Resources is breakeven in the low $2 range, these risks are subdued.

Rexford's biggest risk is a prolonged downturn in market rents and/or its inability to source new deals to grow beyond its existing rent escalators.

data.title: ASML's €1.3B Mistral AI Bet: Strategic Genius Or European Sovereign Gamble?
data.description:
ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) recently announced that it was investing in Mistral AI which I consider a key development for the European AI sphere. In my last report, I marked the stock the best disappointing stock to buy and since then the stock has gained almost 25%. So, definitely not bad for a stock that has disappointed at times due to macro and trade pressures. In this report, however, I don’t want to discuss valuation of ASML but I want to discuss the investment in Mistral AI. To some, the investment in Mistral AI makes no sense but as I explain in this report it makes a lot of sense.

Brief Overview Of The €1.3 Billion Investment In Mistral AI

ASML will be investing €1.3 billion in Mistral AI representing an 11% ownership in Mistral AI and giving it one seat on the company’s Strategic Committee. The company will explore opportunities to leverage AI across ASML products, R&D, development and faster time to market and higher efficiency of the lithography machines.

ASML’s AI Investment Is Only A Partial Break From The Past

ASML’s AI investment seems like a break from the past. In the past, the company mostly acquired business or invested in companies with focus on supply chain and technology. In 2012, the company acquired Cymer for light-source technology followed by the acquisition of Hermes Microvision in 2016 to strengthen inspection capabilities and in 2020 the company acquired Berliner Glas Group to acquire optical glass and optics components. In 2016, the company acquired a minority stake in Carl Zeiss SMT to lay the foundation for the development of the next generation of EUV machines. So, all these acquisitions and investments strengthened the company’s technology capability and in some way were also necessary to keep R&D and production ramp up for EUV and High NA at the pace that the company requires.

The investment in Mistral AI is different in the sense that prior acquisitions and investments were focused on hardware solutions and technologies, and the Mistral AI investment is AI. Prior investments and acquisitions were also much closer related to lithography and supply chain control to align EUV development. The artificial intelligence investment is much more broad-based. The investment is not driven by any desire of ASML to capitalize on AI opportunities that Mistral AI may have with other customers, but it is squarely focused on implementing AI through critical cycles. Those are the R&D cycle, production cycle and operational cycle.

In that sense, there also is a similarity between the AI investment and the past acquisitions and investments, namely that ASML’s investment in Mistral AI is aligned with the core business, and I believe that similarity overarches all dissimilar elements. In the past, ASML invested and acquired to gain access critical technology and capabilities and that is also the case now even though it may appear less critical for any specific product.

Why Investing Makes Sense Rather Than Procuring

One question that investors may have right now is why ASML decided to invest in Mistral AI rather than procuring a service. The answer is actually quite simple. For €1.3 billion, the company will get a seat in the Strategic Committee of Mistral AI. When a service is procured, you simply procure the service and that is it. By getting a seat on the committee, ASML can make sure Mistral AI’s development direction is aligned with ASML’s core strategic objectives for deployment of AI. So, the €1.3 billion does not only get the company access to AI models it also gets the company a say in the development path and priorities of Mistral AI and that better safeguards Mistral AI’s fit in ASML’s core objectives.

Why Not Invest In Other AI Companies Such as OpenAI?

One may wonder why ASML was interested in a stake in Mistral AI while there are other more capable options; If we look at the leaderboard for language learning models or LLMs, Mistral AI definitely does not have a good an impressive rating. Open AI’s models, Grok, Claude and Gemini rank significantly higher than Mistral’s most advanced model. However, this is not about getting access to the most advanced model but getting access to a company that is willing to develop in the direction of ASML’s desires. ASML will get an 11% stake for €1.3 billion. That values the company at €11.8 billion or around $14 billion. OpenAI is currently looking at a securing funding that would value OpenAI at $500 billion. For ASML, to get even a 10% stake to somewhat steer development direction it would need to pay at least $50 billion. So, ASML would have to pay 32.3x the Mistral AI investment to have the weight in OpenAI and while OpenAI models lead the way, OpenAI models are not 32x more capable. So, from a dollar-value against performance perspective, the Mistral AI investment is the better choice even more so since it is highly likely that Mistral will develop more capable models tailored for ASML.

The other reason probably comes down to the European sovereignty angle. ASML’s investment backs a European AI champion. Europe is many steps behind on AI and this could be a significant boost to Europe’s AI capabilities and ultimately boost the value of ASML’s investment.

Apart from that, ASML has been caught up by US imposed export controls on lithography machines and as a result I do believe that the company also tries to be less reliant on the US as the company knows that any infusion of AI models from US developers could in the future potentially result in export control of products and services. Furthermore, the political bond between the US and Europe has substantially weakened and in the same way European governments and companies are trying to become more self-sufficient for defense and security needs, the same also holds for artificial intelligence and chips.

Conclusion: Mistral AI Investment Makes More Sense Than You Think

While the investment in Mistral AI somewhat deviates from prior acquisitions and investments made by ASML. I believe that the investment follows the same strategic rationale of ASML. Furthermore, we note that the selection of Mistral AI for investment makes sense since from dollar value perspective it may be a more rewarding investment for ASML and Mistral AI is a European company and in that sense the investment also fits in an approach that somewhat favors European companies amidst a very protectionist stance in the US for the past years, which has significantly affected ASML’s business. So, from multiple angles I believe there are more weighted elements for this investment to make sense than there are elements that make the investment an odd one. Solely viewing the investment through the scope of an AI leaderboard position on model strength ignores the many elements that go into this decision.

I believe that the investment has a lot of potential for ASML and functions as a building block for ASML to develop next generation capabilities, do so in accelerated fashion and drive output efficiency while derisking political elements from the scene. I have a background in aerospace and one of the complex parts was MDO or multidisciplinary optimization and I believe that AI can definitely be leveraged to significantly enhance and optimize MDO methods to converge to better working solutions faster.

So, I remain bullish on ASML even after a significant increase in the stock price since my last coverage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UIVM: This Hidden Dividend ETF Has Quietly Beaten The S&P 500
data.description:
Introduction

Although the U.S. economy appears to be weakening, with the August jobs report, the S&P 500 (SP500) has continued to make new-highs, making bargains in the market scarce.

While I do think there are some decent deals in the REIT (XLRE) sector, investors may have to look elsewhere for potential bargains right now.

That elsewhere may be outside of the U.S. If this is you, then I may have a bargain for you with this particular ETF. Additionally, they sport a solid 3.84% yield and have crushed the S&P 500 in the past year.

And at a P/E of just 13.96x at the time of writing, income-oriented investors looking for portfolio diversification, VictoryShares International Value Momentum ETF (NASDAQ:UIVM) may be a hidden gem. Additionally, this is attractive when compared to the S&P's P/E of nearly 26x.

In this article I'll dive into this undercovered ETF and discuss why they may be a good fit for your portfolio, particularly if you're a retiree.

Who Is UIVM?

VictoryShares International Value Momentum ETF is internationally focused and looks to track the Nasdaq Dev Market ex-US Large Mid Cap Index. UIVM has an inception date of October 24, 2017 and looks to give investors exposure in non-U.S. equity markets by providing (exposure) to value & momentum factors.

What I found particularly attractive at first glance about UIVM was the reasonable expense ratio of 0.35%, dividend yield closer to 4%, and their performance vs. the index, which I'll delve into later. Currently, the ETF has 186 holdings.

Below is a list of their top 10 (holdings). Because the ETF focuses on international equities, the only company I was familiar with was Enbridge Inc. (ENB), the Canadian Midstream company.

You also see one of their top 10 holdings is Power Corporation of Canada (POW:CA), a life & health insurance company based out of Montreal. Canada makes up 8.96% of UIVM's portfolio, while Japan and the United Kingdom make up the largest portions at 16.65% and 10.58%, respectively.

Other notable companies that investors may be familiar with are Honda Motor Company (HMC), British American Tobacco (BTI), Schindler (OTC:SHLRF), Volkswagen AG (OTCPK:VWAGY), and Rolls-Royce Holdings (OTCPK:RYCEY).

Germany and South Korea round out the top 5 at 9.09% and 8.60%. Regarding UIVM's regional diversification, most of the portfolio is concentrated in Europe at 54.78%. Asia-Pacific makes up 34.71%, and North America is at nearly 9%.

Performance

Over the past year, UIVM has outperformed the S&P in price returns, up over 24% compared to roughly 17.32%.

Over the past 3 years, they've also outperformed Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO), with UIVM up over 82% compared to 78.31% for VOO.

And UIVM's strong performance is likely due to their NAV growth, which has grown nicely over the past 1, 3, and 5 years.

You can see from the chart above that their NAV is up roughly 24% in the past year alone. Over 3- and 5-year periods, their NAV has grown 17.07% and 12.45%, respectively.

And while past performance is no indication of future performance, UIVM may be in for continued success if Goldman Sachs (GS) economists are correct.

According to a study in 2023, emerging economies are projected to grow faster than developed markets by 2056. If so, it's possible UIVM could continue outperforming the S&P over the longer term.

Starting 3.84% Yield Attractive

Because UIVM's portfolio is concentrated mostly in financials & industrials, the ETF pays a solid starting yield of 3.84%.

Moreover, the fact that they pay on a monthly basis instead of quarterly makes them more appealing than international ETFs like Schwab U.S. International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY) and Vanguard International High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYMI), both of which pay quarterly.

Both SCHY & VYMI do have lower expense ratios at just 0.08% and 0.17%, respectively. And while all three have decent starting yields closer to 4%, UIVM's monthly distributions may be a deciding factor if you're more income-focused, like a retiree.

Moreover, UIVM has outperformed both ETFs in total returns over the past year, up over 30% compared to 12.48% for SCHY and nearly 24% for VYMI.

Over a 3-year period, they've also outperformed, up over 82% compared to a respectable 72.54% for VYMI and 50.41% for SCHY.

In comparison to their direct peers, UIVM has outperformed the entire group the past 3 years with the exception of Fidelity International Value Factor ETF (FIVA) & FlexShares Morningstar Developed Markets ex-US Factor Tilt Index Fund ETF (TLTD), up 63.39% and 59.92%, respectively.

According to data, UIVM's last distribution was paid for $0.52, paid on the 9th of September. This was their highest distribution for 2025 aside from June's $0.53. Another metric that may make this ETF appealing to retirees is their distributions are considered ordinary income instead of qualified like many peers.

This means they are better held in a tax-advantaged account. Furthermore, because economists are predicting emerging markets growth will outpace developed markets in the next 30 years, international exposure in your portfolio may be worth it to pass on to your heirs.

P/E of 13.96x Attractive Vs. U.S. Equities

Aside from their yield, UIVM has a P/E of just 13.96x. Peers Fidelity International Value Factor ETF and Global X MSCI SuperDividend EAFE ETF (EFAS) have lower P/Es of 12.13x and 10.72x.

However, FIVA has a lower dividend yield at 2.93%, while EFAS' has a higher yield closer to 5%. But EFAS has a higher expense ratio, 20 basis points higher at 0.55%.

Furthermore, international funds and individual stocks trade at much more attractive discounts when comparing them to the S&P's P/E of nearly 26x.

Underperformance Risks

Although UIVM has outperformed the S&P over the past 1 and 3 years, they have underperformed the index over a 5-year period. And going forward, this could continue, especially if President Trump is successful with getting businesses to invest stateside.

With less than 10 years since its inception, investors may wonder how the ETF will do over the longer-term and prefer to invest in more notable names like SCHY and VYMI.

Investor Takeaway

VictoryShares International Value Momentum has outperformed the S&P over the past 1 and 3 years, a testament to their potential quality.

Moreover, with a dividend yield above 4% and monthly distribution, they may be a suitable investment for income-oriented investors like retirees.

Furthermore, their international focus, reasonable valuation, and outperformance should be considered if you're looking for diversification outside of U.S. equities for the long-term.

While international stocks could continue to underperform U.S. stocks despite Goldman Sachs economists predicting emerging markets will outpace developed markets over the next 3 decades, consider UIVM if you're a retiree looking for a solid starting yield and international exposure.

data.title: Apple: Ignore The Noise, iPhone 17 Could Be The Catalyst Bulls Waited For
data.description:
The iPhone 17 unveil may not have given Wall Street a "wow" because upgrades felt incremental, not breakthrough, and pricing failed to excite investors, but I think this new device underlines the reason Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) continues to be a long-term winner. A new collection based on the A19 chip, wired with Apple Intelligence, demonstrates how the company can continue to develop its flagship product and further enhance graphical methods to integrate AI into its market niche.

Although the stock has gone back short-term due to the quiet response by investors, Apple's fundamentals are still good, the sales of iPhones are at a record high, service sales are growing, available cash is strong, and the balance sheet still supports buybacks. The sensitivity in valuations is found in analyst debate, yet with Apple having a premium brand, lock-in ecosystem, and a 1.5 billion built-in user base of iPhones, the company enjoys a potent upgrade cycle in the next few years.

In fact, Evercore ISI calls the slim iPhone Air “truly different”, a potential MacBook Air moment for iPhones, and says it could kick-start a long-overdue upgrade wave.

Similarly, Wedbush sees the new phones as the “spark for a new era,” noting some 300+ million iPhone owners haven’t upgraded in over four years.

The iPhone 17 event of Apple revolves around a single near-term battle point, the new iPhone Air. And in this article I am developing a high- narrow, testable thesis: should the Air be able to convince even a relatively small percent of the 300 or 320 million individuals that bought iPhones many cycles back, yet have not reinvested to date, to trade up again Apple will get to record a quantifiably and possibly even sizeably increase in device revenues, services pull-through and services margins; should it not it will once again revert to services growth and buybacks to keep the returns flowing.

The individual notion that the Air is the getting-upgrading catalyst is decomposed below with the metrics to make valuation, weight-of-real-world upside, and the indicator investors must monitor to discern a real cycle, versus places where there is advertising blarf.

Why the Air could matter (The Mechanism)

Air is a mechanical case because of its addressable scale. According to Webush, "approximations of 300-320 million iPhone users out of a total of 1.5 billion have not been upgraded in several cycles". That concentrated cohort is an exceptionally valuable group of customers in the sense that a small percentage of them convert to high unit volumes at Apple.

Second, ASP and mix: In the case of giving Air a quality, design-based new entry that mesmerises customers into moving off older models without extending price averages or increasing them, it gives Apple the benefit of volume as well as an improved mix.

Third, services pull-through: Historically, new device buyers spend more on subscriptions and in-app purchases in the first year of their upgrade; that recurring revenue has a high contribution to margins and valuations.

Quantitative Situation: Abortive Upgrade Results.

In order to render the potential impact real, we can assume three realistic scenarios: in response to the possibility of an upgrade pool ever present of 315 million users, we conservatively assume an initial hypothesis of a blended ASP of about 900 on the newly acquired iPhones. When the proportion of conversion is 5% then, assuming all applicants are bears, Apple sells approximately 15.75 million units and generates a revenue of 14.2 billion of incremental device revenue.

In the case of a 10% conversion, Apple will sell approximately 31.5 million units; it will add approximately $28.4 billion. When the conversion per 20% is a bull case, Apple is able to move about 63.0 million units, making it about 56.7 billion. Even those one-time device revenue numbers are huge compared to a single quarter, such as the Q3 at $94 billion, and explain how even a small conversion rate of an enormous installed base can change the needle.

Layer in services: assuming a 5% growth in the number of services recovered (attracted) by growth in revenue $27.4 billion, then there goes an incremental $1.4 billion revenue/year in constant growth.

The punch: even small and realistic changes in conversion and attach rates could lead to disproportionate effects on top line and, via free cash flow and buybacks, on EPS and multiple expansion.

Apple is Catching Up to Rivals in AI

Apple isn’t just banking on hardware. Over the past year, it has bet big on AI – or as Apple calls it, Apple Intelligence. CEO Tim Cook openly said the company is “significantly growing” its investments in AI and even reallocating teams to focus on it.

In practice, this means stuffing new AI-powered features into iPhones, Macs, and iPads: smarter Siri, live language translation on AirPods, generative image tools, and AI-driven photo editing, to name a few. Early results are promising. Apple recently reported that markets where its new AI features went live saw stronger iPhone sales; CEO Cook noted that the 16 iPhone models in those regions outperformed those in places without Apple Intelligence.

In other words, consumers seem to be upgrading their phones to get those AI goodies.

Services, too, are getting an AI boost. Apple’s record services revenue, already the fastest-growing part of its business, is being turbocharged by AI. For example, new “Writing Tools” and “Image Playground” features in apps like Notes and Photos are keeping customers glued to Apple’s ecosystem. Zacks analysts highlight that Apple’s recent quarter saw double-digit growth not just in the iPhone business, but also in Mac and Services, and they credit Apple Intelligence as a key driver behind that trend.

Apple Beats Q3 Earnings Estimate

In Apple's latest quarter, Q3 2025, the company reported a record $94 billion in revenue, a healthy 10% jump year-over-year. At the earnings release, CEO Tim Cook pointed out that each product category achieved single-digit gains: a doubling character in iPhone and Mac, and double-digit gains in Services led to this all-time quarter, as well as overall geographical expansion. EPS under adjusted basis also exceeded the analysts' estimate at $1.57, which is 12% of that of last year.

Here’s the breakdown of segments: iPhone sales climbed roughly high single-digits (Apple’s “cash cow” remains the largest chunk of revenue), thanks in part to strong demand for the iPhone 16 lineup. Mac sales rebounded 15% with the new M4 chip models, a fresh all-time high for the June quarter. Services (App Store, iCloud, etc.) hit an all-time record of about $27.4 billion, roughly 13% YoY growth.

Apple's balance sheet is as solid as ever. It held tens of billions of cash and short-term investments prior to the quarter. At Q3 2025, Apple possessed nearly $36 billion in cash, in addition to cash equivalents. Having total assets of more than $330 billion and nascent net debt, the tech giant is generating huge free cash flow. It would imply plenty of firepower to issue shareholder returns (dividends and buybacks) and to make strategic decisions in AI or others.

Markets

Speaking of the markets, Apple still is a market leader in the U.S. markets and, more to the point, the world markets but China has turned out a deceiver.

Apple holds the largest market share of approximately 62% in the worldwide market in the upmarket segment of Smartphones, although teards have pecked Huawei and Xiaomi, it continues to dominate the target market (Hammons, 2018).

Indeed, by 2025, in China, Huawei regained the leadership in smartphone delivery with an estimated 18% market share, and Apple shipments increased by a mere 4% compared to 2020 and ranked fifth in the China market. With a fresh sign of how intense the competition is (Apple even shut down a mainland China store this year, the first in years), it closed it. The sales of Apple in China are about to stabilize due to government subsidies on consumer electronics, but the company is trailing other local brands.

This would put a full-year 2025 growth at approximately that rate. Concisely, Apple is not anticipating a blow-out growth using iPhone 17 (and the holiday season) but relying on consistent increases in revenue to achieve the goals through Services.

For the future, Apple projected mid to high single-digit revenue growth as of the fourth quarter in 2025. The group of analysts concurs with this view: analysts at Zacks are forecasting a fiscal Q4 (Sept quarter) of roughly $100.8 billion, 6% higher than in 2024. It was a quick growth that would reach full-year 2025 at such a pace.

Arguably, in brief, Apple is not hoping for a blow-out growth right now; it assumes a gradual increase in iPhone 17 (and during the holiday season) and continued strength in Services to hit those targets.

Valuation

Apple currently trades at a forward P/E of around 31× and an EV/EBITDA of near 25×. By comparison, Microsoft’s (MSFT) forward P/E is 32× (EV/EBITDA 24×) and Nvidia’s is much higher (40× P/E, EV/EBITDA 39×)

In other words, Apple’s multiples are below Nvidia’s and roughly in line with MSFT’s, suggesting the stock is undervalued compared to most of its other "Magnificent 7" members.

Growth- pipeline: iPhone analysts predict slower growth (Analysts project 7% revenue CAGR for the next 7 years). The increase in iPhone sales is expected to be in the form of new models and a premium mix (Apple sees 6% iPhone growth, services 10%+). Apple also continues to improve its product offering, e.g., releasing Vision Pro with a faster M4 chip, a new strap, and ultimately AR glasses, and introducing on-device AI (was initially part of Vision Pro) to both iPhone, Mac, Watch, and Vision Pro. These would fuel an increase in services and hardware.

Effects on device gross-margin: Hardware is forecasted to have a lower marginal than services; however, higher ASPs and a good mix ratio toward Pro devices can boost aggregate gross margin. Services growth is the most impactful, but it, at the same time, has the best gross margins, and elevated recurring profit intensifies operating leverage to release additional cash straight to buybacks and enhance the discussion of a premium multiple.

On the other hand, failure to upgrade in time could place the company under the potential of fast multiple reduction since even the large-cap peers already trade at premiums compared to Apple.

Risks to Consider

The complete analysis is impossible without a dose of reality. In case the iPhone 17 does not gather an upgrade rush, Apple has a complex risk profile. Reduced device sales would tighten near-term revenue and halo the supporting higher-margin projects and services of both App Store and iCloud, and subscriptions, slowing the overall margin growth.

The mood of investors, which may narrow the stock premium, might change, and buybacks would not be so effective as they support the share price. It would put pressure on future prices as competitors would have a hint to escape defections and speed up feature parity.

Reduced refresh cycles also put pressure on supply-chain planning, and Apple was exposed to either inventory or contractual costs.

Lastly, a temperate launch would motivate Apple to expedite software or pricing remedies, accelerate the program of subsequent products, which would impact the brand momentum, which would reduce brand momentum which in turn would Kuwait the execution risk in cases where the growth projections are huge.

Conclusion

So what’s the bottom line for retail investors? Apple is still a benign, blue-chip stock on the market. It’s proven it can reinvent itself (from PCs to phones to wearables to services) and has a massive, sticky user base. The iPhone 17 family and its Air as a core nexus are the only near-term catalysts that can be implemented by the Apple investors who intend to have a well-defined thesis. Provided the Air triggers an authentic, quantifiable upgrade cascade by the sizable base of non-dominating our market of lagging iPhone holders, Apple can record device revenue gains and services pulling through that can count multiple times over.

In casual terms, if you love Apple’s stuff and believe in its ecosystem, this stock probably still belongs in your portfolio. The company’s balance sheet is fortress-strong, and it continues to return cash to shareholders. The short-term volatility (even after big launches) can be annoying, but over the long haul, Apple has consistently delivered. Just remember the risks: don’t assume instant, blockbuster returns. This is more of a marathon than a sprint.

For most retail investors, Apple remains a core long-term holding, with the new iPhones and AI plans as reasons to stay optimistic if you’re in it for the big picture.

data.title: The Near-Perfect Portfolio Of 3-13% Yields For The Fed's Rate Cuts
data.description:
The Federal Reserve just cut interest rates by 25 basis points and adjusted the dot plot to signal two more rate cuts to come this year and several next year. Normally, this sort of news would prompt a strong rally in bonds (BND) and bond-proxy sectors like real estate investment trusts (VNQ) and utilities (XLU). While it may do that in the short term, I believe ultimately this move is going to backfire on its intended purpose and will actually send long-term interest rates higher, as I discussed in depth in a recent article.

With my view that these rate cuts are premature and could cause inflation to pick up as well as long-term interest rates to remain higher for longer, I am going to share a model portfolio for how you can position your portfolio to thrive in the current environment.

Income-Friendly Sectors I Like

The sectors that I like most in the current environment that also generate income include gold mining stocks (GDX) that pay dividends, gold and silver bullion leases, midstream companies, infrastructure companies (UTF) with inflation protections built in, certain types of REITs, and perhaps rounding out the portfolio with some broadly diversified blue-chip dividend stocks.

Gold & Silver Plays

There are plenty of blue-chip gold mining stocks that pay dividends, but none of them are particularly attractive at the moment. Endeavour Mining plc (OTCQX:EDVMF) does not appear particularly cheap after its tremendously strong performance year-to-date, though it does pay a pretty attractive dividend yield. I also like B2Gold (BTG), as I see it as one of the more attractively priced miners, although it too has had a tremendous rally this year, so it is not nearly as cheap as it was when we were loading up on shares earlier this year around $2.50 per share.

Meanwhile, you can certainly buy gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) ETFs and sell calls against them to generate income, but then you are capping your upside. Additionally, you are paying an expense ratio for those funds, which further erodes returns. In contrast, I prefer investing in gold and silver bullion leases that give you full ownership of the underlying metal. That way you get to participate fully in the upside, avoid paying an expense ratio, and still generate around a 4% yield from the lease payments. These leases tend to be low risk since they are fully insured and never actually sit on the balance sheet of the counterparty. In that sense, they are lower risk than loans such as gold and silver bonds, though those can also be attractive forms of income if you are willing to take on a bit more risk.

Midstream Opportunities

Meanwhile, in the midstream space, there are a plethora of attractive opportunities, but generally I want ones that are well diversified and include exposure to natural gas and/or NGL export facilities, which can enable them to grow quite well thanks to the tailwinds of reindustrialization, the AI boom, and the U.S. NGL export boom. This should help offset any headwinds that might come from a recession. In addition, I want ones with strong balance sheets that can remain relatively immune to a higher-for-longer interest rate environment, while also paying attractive dividends and having a high likelihood of growing those payouts at a pace that beats inflation moving forward.

You can accomplish this by simply investing in an ETF like the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and the Global X MLP ETF (MLPA), as they both offer dividend yields near 8% and should be growing them at a mid-single-digit pace while giving broad diversification across the sector with high concentration in leading blue chips. Alternatively, if you want to deal with K-1s, you can invest in the likes of Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and MPLX (MPLX). All three of those offer good diversification across NGLs, natural gas, and oil-related midstream businesses and commodities, while also paying a very high yield ranging from 7% to 8% on a forward-looking basis with very strong balance sheets and attractive growth profiles that should lead to mid- to high-single-digit distribution growth over the next five years. Moreover, they all generate their cash flows primarily from contracted assets with limited commodity price exposure and therefore should be fairly recession-resistant.

AI-Linked Infrastructure

I also like AI-related infrastructure with inflation protections built in because, similar to midstream, the powerful macro tailwind from AI demand should help offset slowdowns from recessionary forces. Moreover, the inflation protections and strong balance sheets should help it weather a higher-for-longer inflation and long-term interest rate environment that I am concerned the Fed’s rate cuts will promote. I really like alternative asset managers that have significant exposure in infrastructure, like Brookfield Asset Management (BAM)(BN), and I also like individual infrastructure plays within the Brookfield Empire, such as Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP). BIP and BEP have significant exposure to AI tailwinds, very strong balance sheets, and the vast majority of their cash flows are either inflation-indexed or inflation-protected. In addition, if you want to avoid the K-1s that BIP and BEP issue, you can invest in their economic equivalents, Brookfield Infrastructure Corporation (BIPC) and Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC). TC Energy (TRP), although it is mostly a midstream company, could fit this category given that it has exposure to nuclear power and significant natural gas exposure in close proximity to data centers, while also having good inflation protections on its cash flows. Enbridge (ENB) is another AI infrastructure opportunity, albeit not one that I like quite as much as TRP, BIP, or BEP right now.

REITs That Hold Up Better

Finally, while some REITs like Realty Income (O) and Agree Realty (ADC), I believe, are not going to thrive as much as some may expect in a rate-cutting environment—since they have little in the way of inflation-indexed cash flows and have long-duration leases that make them bond proxies—I think plays like W. P. Carey (WPC) are better suited because about half of their rental revenue comes from leases with CPI-linked escalators while still maintaining a very strong balance sheet and defensive portfolio. You can also invest in multi-family REITs like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) and AvalonBay Communities (AVB), given that they are fairly defensive in nature due to the essential nature of residential real estate and have strong balance sheets with low leverage, coupled with their short-term leases that allow them to keep up with inflation and handle a higher-for-longer interest rate environment better than other REITs.

Broad Dividend Exposure

To round out the portfolio, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and a fund like the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) or the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI) (which comes with a hefty 13%+ TTM yield) both give investors exposure to a broad swath of blue-chip stocks, including mega-cap tech, while still generating an attractive return. While I do not expect these companies in particular to thrive in this environment, the fact that they are blue-chip companies with lengthy track records of growing earnings and, in many cases, dividends year after year through all sorts of economic environments makes them fairly stable core holdings through an uncertain and potentially stagflationary environment.

What I Am Avoiding

One sector that I am especially minimizing exposure to is the business development company (BIZD) space, as places like Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) and Main Street Capital (MAIN) do not look particularly attractively priced right now and are likely to suffer from weakening underwriting performance amid economic weakness, alongside falling short-term interest rates and rising long-term interest rates, which is my expectation. I recently detailed why BDCs are a poor choice right now in this article.

Investor Takeaway

While it is possible that in the near term these markets may perform, I believe that once it becomes evident that the war on inflation is not over and that the government is not cutting spending, long-term interest rates are likely to remain higher or potentially even rise further, while the economy will likely continue to flounder for the foreseeable future. This should bode much better for the stagflation-resistant opportunities that I mentioned in this article than some of the other alternatives out there. Therefore, these are the types of investments that I am loading up on in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Japan's Inflection Point
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

Over the past several months, Japan has quietly gone from global laggard to one of the most intriguing bright spots in global equities. The numbers make the case. While most peers have delivered steady but unremarkable returns, Japan's acceleration stands out. In figure 1:

  • Japan small caps are the clear standout in 2025: They lead all major groups over 6 months (+23.1%), 12 months (+21.6%) and year-to-date (+24.8%). The leadership is consistent and broad-based, showing that small-cap Japan has been the strongest equity segment globally.
  • Japan large caps also strong: Japan is solidly positive across time frames (+16.7% over 6 months, +12.9% over 12 months, +16.9% YTD) but trails its small-cap peers. The story is one of meaningful strength, yet not quite at the level of Japan small caps.
  • Momentum accelerating into 2025: Both large and small caps in Japan are near the top of the global tables over the latest 1–3 months (Japan small +4.5% and Japan +4.4% in 1 month; Japan small +9.7% and Japan +9.6% in 3 months). This suggests Japan's equity rally is not fading—it's gaining fresh traction.

Markets often turn on inflection points, and Japan is beginning to look like one of those shifts in trajectory.

What makes this surge compelling isn't only the absolute returns, but the relative positioning. Compare Japan with the U.S., commonly associated with the term "US exceptionalism." In figure 1, the U.S. is visible in the lower half of the chart for most of the time periods.

Figure 1: Japan Surges, U.S. Stalls: Shifting Leadership in Global Markets

Professionally Managing a Concept from Warren Buffett

The WisdomTree Japan Opportunities Fund (OPPJ) began in July 2025, and the timing was deliberate. Japan is not simply in a rebound cycle; it is undergoing a structural re-rating of its corporate culture. At the center of the strategy are the five sogo shosha, Itochu, Marubeni, Mitsubishi, Mitsui, and Sumitomo.1 These trading houses are more than conglomerates. They are diversified ecosystems spanning energy, commodities, logistics, finance and consumer markets, miniature versions of Berkshire Hathaway itself.

Warren Buffett has singled them out for their rare blend of traits: steady dividend growth, opportunistic buybacks and management teams that display notable restraint in executive compensation. In a country long criticized for corporate inertia, Buffett's endorsement marked a turning point.2

And this wasn't a passing comment. In both the 2023 and 2024 Berkshire letters, Buffett made clear that stakes in the five houses, now roughly 9% of each,3 were meant to be permanent. "Forever" is a concept Buffett has historically reserved for what he views as some of the strongest businesses, two examples of which could be Coca-Cola and American Express.4 To apply it to Japanese firms acquired only in 2019 speaks volumes about Berkshire's conviction.

Apple vs. Japan: A Tale of Two Philosophies

This makes the juxtaposition with Apple (AAPL) all the more fascinating. Apple has been one of the greatest trades in Berkshire's history. From a cost basis of about $36 billion beginning in 2016, Berkshire amassed more than 900 million shares at its peak. By mid-2025, it had sold well over $100 billion worth of Apple stock, locking in profits on a scale rarely seen in market history. Even after these sales, Berkshire still owns roughly 280 million shares worth $57.4 billion, making Apple its single largest equity holding.5

Yet, the philosophy has shifted, as is visible in figure 2. Apple's core engine, the iPhone, has plateaued. Services and other hardware have taken on more importance, but overall revenues have leveled off. The chart below tells the story clearly: Apple's once-relentless growth curve has flattened, and the dependence on iPhone sales remains hard to ignore.

Figure 2: Apple's Revenue Machine: Plateau after a Decade of Expansion

Buffett and Abel are signaling something profound. Apple, once described as untouchable, is clearly no longer a forever holding. Japan's trading houses now carry that designation.

Valuation Divergence

Buffett's Japan purchases began on July 4, 2019, the baseline for measuring how valuations have evolved.6 Since then, Apple's price-to-earnings multiple has exploded from 11.6 times at entry to about 35 times today. The sogo shosha? Their valuations have barely budged, in some cases even compressing, as is visible in figure 3.

This divergence is the crux of the opportunity. Apple's success has already been priced in. Japan's trading houses, despite Berkshire's involvement and improving fundamentals, remain conservatively valued. Figure 3 makes it plain: Apple's multiple has tripled, while Japan's remains stubbornly cheap.

Figure 3: Valuations Tell the Story: Apple Stretched, Japan Still Undervalued

OPPJ: Democratizing Buffett's Play

Here's the catch: Berkshire itself is capped. It cannot push ownership in the trading houses beyond 9.9% without regulatory hurdles. That ceiling locks Buffett out of buying more, even if his conviction deepens. OPPJ faces no such limits. With roughly 45% of its weight in the "Buffett Basket" of the five Japanese trading houses, it gives investors access to the very exposure Buffett wishes he could own at a greater scale.

But OPPJ goes further. The portfolio layers in three additional pillars, of corporate governance improvers, capital-return champions and thematic opportunities. These complement the trading houses with a broader set of Japanese companies that are buying back stock, raising dividends and aligning compensation with shareholder value. This is not a cultural aspiration anymore. It's happening. Shareholder payouts in Japan have quadrupled over the past decade, with buybacks alone now eclipsing the total dividends of just 10 years ago.

Beyond Buffett: Fundamentals in Motion

While the Buffett Basket anchors OPPJ, the strategy intentionally looks beyond. Japan's transformation is visible in its fundamentals.

Figure 4 highlights cumulative earnings per share growth since 2012 across major developed markets. Japan's trajectory is striking, surpassing both Europe and the U.K., and narrowing the gap with the U.S. The so-called "Japan discount" persists, but the earnings power is real and rising.

Figure 4: Earnings Power Reshapes the Japan Narrative

The second chart shows dividends. Over the same period, Japan's dividend growth has outpaced peers by a wide margin. Once criticized for hoarding cash, Japanese companies are now returning capital with discipline and consistency. Investors are no longer waiting for governance reforms to show up in numbers, they already have.

Figure 5: From Cash Hoards to Cash Back: Japan's Dividend Surge

Conclusion: What's Priced In?

Going back to Apple, we'd clearly note that we don't see a revenue problem. We do see a valuation in the range of 35 times earnings that we believe has an inherent expectation of future growth that may not materialize. At a lower valuation, it could represent an opportunity.

We've seen the five trading houses currently held by Berkshire and major exposures within OPPJ, and we have seen the overall earnings and dividend growth in Japan relative to other markets. It's clear that there are positive fundamentals, but the question is, what is priced in?

Figure 6 showcases the equity risk premium, which is defined as the earnings yield of a given index minus the 10-year real interest rate for that market.

  • The 10-Year inflation-adjusted Japan government bond yield is subtracted from OPPJ's and the MSCI Japan Index's earnings yields to indicate the equity risk premiums for these Japan equity universes.
  • The 10-Year TIPS interest rate is subtracted from the S&P 500 Index's earnings yield to indicate the equity risk premium for this widely followed U.S. index universe.

You can multiply the equity risk premium of the S&P 500 Index by three and still not quite reach the OPPJ figure, a stark contrast. Many may believe that Japan's equities should have a lower valuation than those of the U.S., but this is quite a significant margin. It could signal an opportunity.

Figure 6: Equity Risk Premiums Diverge: Japan Offers More than 3x the U.S.

The Bigger Picture

Taken together, the case is compelling. Apple's extraordinary run has rewarded Berkshire handsomely, but its valuation leaves little room for compounding. Japan, by contrast, offers discounted valuations, shareholder-friendly reforms and global relevance, ingredients that echo Buffett's favorite playbook of buying wonderful businesses at fair prices and holding them indefinitely.

That's the essence of OPPJ. It professionally replicates Buffett's Japan strategy, while broadening the lens to capture a country in the midst of an earnings and dividend renaissance. In a world of stretched U.S. multiples and crowded trades, Japan stands out as a market where patience, discipline and valuation still converge.

Citations

  1. As of 8/21/25, Itochu, Marubeni, Mitsubishi, Mitsui and Sumitomo had weights of 8.56%, 9.97%, 9.11%, 8.81% and 8.85%, respectively, in OPPJ. Holdings are subject to change.
  2. Sources: "2024 Annual Report," Berkshire Hathaway Inc., 2025; "2023 Annual Report," Berkshire Hathaway Inc., 2024.
  3. Source: "Berkshire Hathaway Raises Stakes in Five Japanese Trading Houses to Near 10%," Reuters, 3/17/35.
  4. Source: J. Baer, "What Warren Buffett Learned from His Biggest Hits, and Misses," The Wall Street Journal, 5/4/25.
  5. Source: E. Kim, "Warren Buffett Hails Tim Cook for Making Berkshire More Money than He Has, after Selling Two-Thirds of His Apple Stake," Business Insider, 5/3/25.
  6. Source: "2023 Annual Report to Shareholders," Berkshire Hathaway Inc., 2024.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund focuses its investments in Japan, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Japan that can adversely affect performance. Investments in currency involve additional special risks, such as credit risk, interest rate fluctuations, derivative investments which can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities, and more sensitive to the effect of varied economic conditions. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers. Due to the investment strategy of this Fund it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.
